title: Intermediaries, Hop-by-Hop Fields, Forwarding, and Transformation · Language Lens: Downstream Upstream Separation
summary: A proxy forwards Connection-nominated fields, copies raw forwarding claims, changes content without metadata repair, and assumes downstream and upstream share one connection and parser. Planned artifact: Add and verify the downstream upstream separation increment in intermediary forwarding and transformation contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Model downstream and upstream connections, framing, versions, deadlines, addresses, TLS, and failures as independent protocol hops.
Remove Connection and every nominated hop-by-hop field before forwarding, then establish fields for the new hop.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Downstream Upstream Separation · Theory Downstream Upstream Separation
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 9801 in a canary edge service advertising HTTP/3 with bounded fallback. Build deterministic Go evidence to strip dynamically nominated hop fields and repair metadata after transformations; preserve this operating constraint: all attacker-controlled counts must be validated before allocation or slicing; then inject Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length and close before any downstream forwarding. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Downstream Upstream Separation contract for the supplied data.

Evidence target: Predict Downstream Upstream Separation · Theory Downstream Upstream Separation.

Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.
